episode_meta.title: Your Questions Answered: A Live Q&A Session in Collaboration with the Online News Association

ai_tools: Perplexity (AI search engine and browser plugin)
Opus Clip (long-form video to short-form clips)
YesSEO (Slack-based AI tool for headlines and SEO metadata)
NOTA (AI tools for publishers: headlines, summaries, article-to-video)
Claude for Sheets / Claude for Docs (Google Workspace plugins)
Adobe Firefly (ethically sourced AI image generation)
Elicit (AI search engine for research papers)
Consensus (AI search engine for research papers)
Data Wrapper (data visualization, mentioned in Wobby workflow)

use_cases.description: Analyzing audience analytics (page views, platform data) by uploading Google Analytics files to ChatGPT and querying in plain language via Code Interpreter
use_cases.outcome: Able to analyze data spread across multiple platforms and produce summaries

use_cases.description: Automated weekly audience analytics reports via no-code AI automation: pulling Google Analytics data, sending to ChatGPT for analysis, and delivering summaries via email or Slack
use_cases.outcome: Scheduled automated analytics summaries (e.g., every Monday morning email or daily Slack updates)

use_cases.description: Podcast data analysis across multiple platforms
use_cases.outcome: ChatGPT's data analysis handles pulling together and analyzing podcast data from multiple platforms

use_cases.description: Three-hour foundational AI literacy courses for newsroom staff in small groups of ~20, followed by prompt clinics (experimentation workshops) pairing editorial and tech staff
use_cases.outcome: Led to product idea 'En Bref' — AI-powered news summary slides on their app

use_cases.description: AI-powered news summary slides ('En Bref') on the Radio Canada app — generative AI helps summarize articles into slide-format news briefs
use_cases.outcome: Reduced production burden on editors who previously had to write slide summaries manually

use_cases.description: AI-generated illustrations for articles as hero images, built into their CMS for editors to prompt
use_cases.outcome: Editors can generate illustrations when no photo is available for a story

use_cases.description: Using AI-generated illustrations (not photorealistic) for editorial content with transparency labeling
use_cases.outcome: Illustrations used as editorial visuals with disclosure

use_cases.description: AI-powered headline suggestions and SEO metadata generation across 8+ languages for local newsrooms via Slack integration
use_cases.outcome: Free tool used by over 400 local newsrooms for headline and SEO optimization

use_cases.description: Repurposing long-form video content into ranked short-form clips for TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts
use_cases.outcome: Converts long-form video into ~20 ranked short clips with improvement feedback

use_cases.description: Searching through thousands of documents (PDFs, handwritten documents, images, emails) for investigative journalism, with text extraction and transcription
use_cases.outcome: AI-powered search across large document collections with OCR for handwritten text

use_cases.description: AI-powered research on scholarly papers — filtering by methodology, time frame; AI-generated abstracts and summaries at a glance; chatting with papers
use_cases.outcome: Rapid filtering and comprehension of research papers for beat reporting

use_cases.description: Connecting to open government data portals, auto-generating AI insights when datasets update, chatting with data, and generating Data Wrapper charts
use_cases.outcome: Democratizes data journalism access for smaller newsrooms with limited data journalists

use_cases.description: Converting articles into short video content (Instagram Reels, shorts) with editing capabilities
use_cases.outcome: Streamlines multi-step video production process from text articles
Used as brainstorming co-pilot; all outputs edited by human

use_cases.description: Generative AI search for quick background research on topics, with follow-up questions and website summarization
use_cases.outcome: Replaces traditional search for quick research; summarizes websites in-browser

key_claims.claim: ChatGPT Teams ($30/month) does not train on user data, making it more appropriate for newsrooms than the free or $20/month paid tiers which do train on chat history
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: We are in a very experimental phase with this technology, and the way to harness it is by understanding what the technology is, what are the right and wrong use cases, and how we can apply it
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: AI ethics policies should be structured around four key components: (1) transparency and disclosure, (2) accuracy and human oversight, (3) privacy and data use, (4) purpose-driven use
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: Newsrooms should create AI principles rather than AI guidelines, because principles address the 'why' and can adapt as technology changes, whereas specific guidelines become outdated quickly
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: Adobe Firefly is a more ethically sourced AI model than Midjourney because it uses Creative Commons images for training
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: Any generative AI tool output currently requires human editing and vetting — nothing should go straight to publish
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: YesSEO is free and used by over 400 local newsrooms for headline suggestions and SEO metadata in more than 8 languages
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: Slack is using user messages to train their AI models and users must opt out manually
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: The fundamental building block for AI in newsrooms is data infrastructure, and most newsrooms still deal with fragmented, inaccessible, or unclean data
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: Nick Thompson (CEO of The Atlantic) argued publisher deals with AI companies can be beneficial because they provide tech expertise, insights, and credits that newsrooms lack
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy (quoting Nick Thompson)

challenges.description: Fear and shame around admitting AI use in newsrooms — some journalists use AI but are afraid to tell colleagues because nobody talks about it openly
Copyright and intellectual property concerns around AI-generated images, particularly tools like Midjourney that can produce copyrighted characters
Trust concerns when AI-generated photorealistic images could be mistaken for actual photographs by audiences
ChatGPT and generative AI tools hallucinate — e.g., making up links — requiring constant human verification
Privacy risks when using AI tools with confidential journalistic data (sources, interviews), especially third-party transcription services
AI vendor evaluation difficulty — companies using jargon loosely (e.g., 'fine-tuned model') to sell products that may not deliver as described
Tool adoption failures due to high learning curves, lack of workflow integration, and stickiness problems
The news industry lacks tech expertise needed to leverage AI, creating dependency on tech companies
Upskilling an entire journalism workforce for the AI revolution — current efforts are still small relative to the scale of the challenge
Data fragmentation across newsrooms — data spread across different sources, inaccessible or unclean, preventing effective AI implementation
Fear in the media industry about partnering with tech platforms, based on historical experience
Competing priorities in newsrooms make it hard to create dedicated space and time for AI experimentation

quotes.text: We are in a very experimental phase with this technology, and the way to harness it is by us understanding what what the technology is, what are the right and wrong use cases of this, and how we can apply it.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy

quotes.text: I use AI a lot, but I'm actually afraid to tell anybody in my team that I do because when nobody's talking about it and I'm just afraid that if I say they they wouldn't understand what I mean by that because they're not hearing anyone else is talking about AI.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy (quoting an anonymous journalist)
quotes.topic: Fear and cultural barriers to AI adoption in newsrooms

quotes.text: It's really important, in my opinion, to be able to know the why we're using AI before even getting to the how we are using AI in the newsroom.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy

quotes.text: I talk about how we can be a one-to-two person newsroom operating as a five person and being able to cover. But what does that mean for the future of work and roles within our newsrooms that is going to be impacted.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy
quotes.topic: Future of work and workforce impact

quotes.text: The basics and building blocks of AI is data and it's about data infrastructure. So one thing that you need to be focusing on right now is making sure that you have good data in your newsroom.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy
quotes.topic: Data infrastructure as foundation for AI

quotes.text: Any generative AI tool that I'm using right now, it has to be edited and it has to be vetted and I wouldn't be taking anything straight to publish. And the way I work I think of using AI tools is this term that's thrown out everywhere is my co-pilot I expect them to take me to a particular level with which after that I add in my own insights and my own knowledge.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Use ChatGPT Teams ($30/month) instead of free or paid tiers for newsroom work, as it does not train on your data and provides enterprise-level privacy
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Build AI literacy through structured training: start with foundational courses on AI basics, then run 'prompt clinics' — experimentation workshops pairing editorial and tech staff
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Create internal Slack channels for 'AI Wins' and 'AI Fails' to build shared institutional knowledge about what works and what doesn't
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Create AI principles (the 'why') rather than detailed AI guidelines (the 'how'), as principles adapt better to rapidly changing technology
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Be transparent about AI use — label AI-generated images with simple disclosures (e.g., 'produced with the help of Adobe Firefly') and choose ethically sourced tools that respect original creators
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: When evaluating AI vendors, examine: who the founding team is, privacy/data usage policies, whether their technical claims are accurate (reverse engineer their process), who their other clients are, and whether the tool fits your workflow with minimal learning curve
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: For confidential interviews, consider running transcription models locally on your computer rather than sending data to third-party AI services
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Start building good data infrastructure now — clean, accessible, unified data — to power future AI models and user experiences for news
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Use Elicit or Consensus for beat reporting that requires reviewing scholarly research papers, especially health/medical beats
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy

recommendations.advice: Follow newsletters like The Wayfinder (Ezra Eaman), The Rundown, and Wonder Tools (Jeremy Kaplan) to stay current on AI developments in media
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy
